questions.question: How do coherent errors accumulate compared to incoherent errors over n gates?
questions.explanation: Coherent errors add linearly (n×ε) because they are systematic and always point the same direction. Incoherent errors add in quadrature (√n×ε) because they are random and partially cancel.

questions.question: What happens to the purity of a quantum state under coherent errors?

options.text: Purity is preserved (remains 1 for pure states)

questions.explanation: Coherent errors are unitary rotations, so they preserve purity. Pure states remain pure (purity = 1). This is in contrast to incoherent errors, which decrease purity by creating mixed states.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of randomized compiling?

options.text: Convert coherent errors to incoherent errors
Reduce the number of gates needed

questions.explanation: Randomized compiling surrounds each gate with random Pauli operators that preserve the logical operation but randomize the frame of the error. This converts systematic coherent errors into random (incoherent) errors that accumulate more slowly.

questions.question: Which is a common physical source of coherent errors?
questions.explanation: Pulse amplitude miscalibration causes systematic over-rotation or under-rotation errors. These are coherent because they occur the same way every time the gate is applied. Thermal photons and spontaneous emission cause incoherent errors.

questions.question: How do coherent errors affect the error correction threshold?

options.text: They decrease the threshold by roughly an order of magnitude
They have no effect on the threshold

questions.explanation: Coherent errors reduce the effective error correction threshold from ~1% to ~0.1% (roughly 10× lower). This is because standard QEC assumes Pauli errors, and coherent errors don't commute nicely with stabilizer measurements.

questions.question: What is ZZ crosstalk in superconducting qubits?

options.text: Always-on coupling that causes conditional phase accumulation
Thermal excitation from the ground state

questions.explanation: ZZ crosstalk is a residual coupling H_ZZ = J_ZZ × Z⊗Z between neighboring qubits. It causes conditional phases to accumulate during idle times: |00⟩ and |11⟩ accumulate opposite phases to |01⟩ and |10⟩.

questions.question: Composite pulse sequences like BB1 can suppress systematic rotation errors by using multiple pulses that cancel out first-order errors.
questions.explanation: True. Composite pulse sequences like SK1 and BB1 use carefully designed pulse combinations that cancel systematic errors. BB1 can suppress rotation errors to higher order, making the residual error scale as ε^n instead of ε.
